title: Expert Systems Fail to Scale, Enterprise Disillusionment Begins
summary: By 1988, the limitations of expert systems had become painfully apparent across industry. Systems were brittle, expensive to maintain, unable to learn from data, and required constant manual knowledge engineering to keep rules current. Companies that had invested millions in expert system deployments found them unable to handle edge cases or adapt to changing conditions. The knowledge acquisition bottleneck — the difficulty of extracting and encoding expert knowledge into rules — proved to be an unsolvable problem within the symbolic paradigm. Corporate AI budgets were slashed dramatically.
impact_description: The expert system crisis discredited symbolic AI in the corporate world for over a decade. AI became a toxic term in business — companies rebranded their AI divisions as 'knowledge systems' or 'advanced computing' to avoid the stigma. This taboo lasted until the deep learning revolution of the 2010s. The failure also validated critics who argued that hand-coded rules could never capture the complexity of real-world knowledge.
counterfactual: If expert systems had scaled gracefully, symbolic AI might have remained the dominant paradigm. The massive shift toward statistical and neural approaches in the 1990s and 2000s was partly driven by the spectacular failure of rule-based systems. Modern LLMs, which learn from data rather than hand-coded rules, exist in part because the expert system approach was proven insufficient.
query_hooks: What is the knowledge acquisition bottleneck?
Why did AI become a dirty word in the 1990s?
What was wrong with symbolic AI?
